These self-modulating microsurgeons outperform all analogues recovered from Xenogen and JoyCo, showing roughly 12 percent higher compatibility scores with plasms. They resist cultivation under laboratory conditions - possibly an anti-theft safeguard. Notably, the nanites exhibit limited autonomy in vivo, suggesting a capacity to rewrite host neural code. This raises the theoretical risk of behavioral alteration or induced docility, reminiscent of abhorrent New Texan psychosexual “Treatment” methods. An indoctrinated [pc.name] Steele? Enhanced cross-species compatibility aligns with OPFOR’s ongoing pursuit of dual-use biotechnology: assets equally applicable to civilian therapy and covert militarization.

These microsurgeons are designed to consume a target from the inside out, weaponizing familiar nanophage technology used in the neutralization of pathogens. Sexual methods of implantation are preferred. A payload of 3,000 phage units is sufficient to consume most humanoids. Consumption may be delayed or targeted towards specific tissues if mission parameters are set prior to implantation.
Biolube cultivation: 65% complete (3 cycles completed thus far).\n\tBeef cultivation: (New Texan White-Blue) 70% complete \n\tAusar Lungfish cultivation: 85% complete

original: Back
Back

“I already told you everything, [pc.name],” Velika replies, each word chosen with surgical care. “Your immune system can splice its own genes to counter unknown pathogens. With a few tweaks, it could become a bioweapon capable of triggering fatal mutations on demand.” “Steele Tech has zero interest in bioweapons,” you say. “We’re not an arms company.” “You’re an inner rim company,” Velika replies, her tone cooling. “And innies aren’t known for their mercy. You still persecute the thraggen, don’t you? Wiped out billions to end one war. Tell me, what wouldn’t you do to end another? A single engineered plague costs less than a bombardment... and leaves the factories standing.” “The war against the thraggen was waged by governments, not corporations,” you counter. “Steele Tech didn’t even exist back then.” Velika exhales sharply, her expression unreadable. “Governments exist to protect corporations, innie. They burn the world so you can sell what’s left of the ashes.”

“I’m a lone wolf,” the cyborg replies, her face an impassive mask. “I acted solely out of patriotic affinity towards the outer rim.” [var0|“Quit bullshitting me. I know you’re lying. I saw some of your memories.”|“I know you’re lying. I saw some of your memories.”] “Then you know it’s not possible for me to tell you anything substantive,” the cyborg replies, a hint of a smirk touching her lips. “I have an infosec system that physically prevents me from ratting. And the real kicker is that it’s rigged to fry my cyberbrain in the event of my death, taking all my secrets to the grave with me.”

The blonde arches an eyebrow at your question. “Been reading my files, eh? Well think about it... the entire point of you participating in the Planet Rush is to mold you into a copy of your father. You might think that you have agency but thus far you’ve been[pc.ra ausar| an obedient puppy] following the script laid out for you to a T: find your company’s probes and become yet another [pc.mf|playboy|playgirl] CEO.” “Another conspiracy theory,” you huff. “That’s hardly proof I’m ‘indoctrinated’ or something.” “Isn’t it?” she asks curtly. “People never outrun what they are, [pc.name]. The only real question is if you’re even capable of doing something against the interest of Steele Tech and the inner rim.”
Love
Love
Tell Velika you love her.
Forgive
Forgive
Tell Velika you forgive her.
Arrest
Arrest
Attempt to arrest Velika.
Kill
Kill
Kill Velika.

The cyborg regards you for a long moment, the vulnerability in her expression giving way to a scoff. “Love? I’m not someone you could love. I’m a monster, innie. And proud of it. There’s no changing me, no ‘fixing’ me... my allegiance is to the ‘rim and it always will be. So spare us both the maudlin bullshit.” You throw your arms around Velika in response, her body going completely rigid as she is drawn into a fierce, possessive hug. After a long moment, a soft chuckle escapes her throat, quickly giving way to a peal of laughter as she throws back her head. “Ahahahahahaha!” Her cackle grows louder, more hysterical as it stretches on and on, echoing throughout the bridge as you stare on in bemused silence. “...you’re far too sweet for your own good, [pc.name],” She finally gasps, her chest heaving against yours. “And... I think I might love you too,” she looks away as she tries to conceal the raw emotion etched across her face. “If you seriously want to try and... be with me... come and find me on Dhaal.” She stands up, her expression totally unreadable. “I want you to have this...” She furnishes you with her old flechetter, thrusting it into your hands. “It’s the least I could do for all the trouble I caused you.” She throws her hands above her head and arches her back in a sensual, feline stretch. “This ship has the coordinates to Uveto already locked in. I’m going to make myself scarce once we arrive... outie.”
Done
Done

The cyborg regards you for a long moment, her expression unreadable. “You... are you serious?” She takes a low, deliberate breath. “I... I really don’t know what to say, [pc.name]. After everything I’ve done... forgiveness is about the last thing I would expect from you. I deserve... far less.” She stands up, seizing you by the shoulders as she wraps you in an abrupt embrace. After a long moment, she draws back and furnishes you with her old flechetter, thrusting it into your hands. “It’s the least I could do for all the trouble I caused you.” She throws her hands above her head and arches her back in a sensual, feline stretch. “This ship has the coordinates to Uveto already locked in. I’m going to make myself scarce once we arrive... outie.”

“Arrest me?” she scoffs “Just so that I can be whored out by some private prison?” Her prosthetic eyes bore into yours. “I’d rather die.” Her hand trembles violently as she raises her flechetter, pressing the cold metal barrel against her temple. “Au revoir.” she pulls the trigger, the gunshot echoing like a clap of thunder as the bullet tears through her skull. She crumples to the floor, her once vibrant hair fanned out around her face as blood pools around her. \n\t\n\tYou look at her for a long moment before retrieving her pistol and punching in the coordinates preset on the bridge’s nav computer, journeying back to Uveto.

Momma Velika will take good care of you...” You barely feel the impact of the dart as your world fades to black, lulled to sleep by chemical bliss.

The smell of cigarettes and cheap perfume assaults your nostrils as you come to sprawled across a duvet in a seedy hotel room, a dull throb emanating from the back of your skull. You somehow know you’re on Tavros but any attempt at ruminating how you got here gives way to an insistent headache. You opt to postpone racking your brains for now, checking your gear before heading out the door.

original: You whisper gentle reassurances as you work gently on the knots of the rope keeping her bound, starting with the two around her mouth and neck. You’re sure to keep physical contact with her, but to keep it gentle: a soft pat on the head, and a squeeze on the shoulder, to let her know that it’s you and that she’s safe. Although her body never stops its quivering, it seems to work, and she becomes visibly calmer over time.

As soon as the last rope keeping her in place is undone, she rolls onto her front, and then she lunges at you, wrapping her limbs around your body and squeezing you tightly to her. original: Although you’re certain it’s working, Nenne can’t help but start crying all over again. The tears stream from her and she buries her face into your chest to hide from the unfamiliarity of her own hold. When you try to stand, she clutches you tighter and whimpers, begging you to remain still, as if where she is right now is the safest place in the whole universe.

You try and reason with Nenne, gently reassuring her that she’s safe, but you need to hurry and hide her before any more Milodan come stumbling into her shop. You’re glad that she’s safe, but there are other Korgonne that need your help, and you can’t do that if Nenne is clinging to you the way she is.

Your words make it through to her, and she steadily releases her iron-tight grip on you, but it happens in ‘sections:’ first her paws lighten their grip on you just a little, and then her limbs unwrap from you just a little, and the process repeats until she’s released from you. All except for her left paw, which has found your way into your hand – she refuses to let that go. “Okay,” she whispers.

The sounds of the Milodan ransacking the hold bounce of the stony walls, making it sound like they’re never too far away. You give her store a sweep, and although it’s certainly a mess after the Milodan had its way with it, there are no undesirables in either her shop or her living area. It’s safe for her here, but you nonetheless tell her to hide somewhere if she can.

“Thank you,” she whispers to you. She leans in close and draws a single, long lick up your cheek, letting you know how much she appreciates you coming to her rescue. “"+({var0}()?"I don’t want to think about what those monsters were going to do–":"Thinking of what monsters doing to Nenne–")+"”

original: In the distance, the sounds of combat are dying down. Rank and file milodans flee and surrender en mass without their leader to keep them organized. The tide has turned, and victory is at hand.
Aftermath

Of course, in the wake of the battle for Korg’ii Hold, quite a mess was left was behind. Wounds had to be tended to. Barricades had to be cleared. Damage to the hold itself had to be cleaned or repaired. Celebrations had to be planned, and an inventory of captives and injuries had to be made.

Somehow, casualties were blessedly few. The milodans came planning to subdue the populace for use in slave labor - and the huge shipments of medicines they brought with them made it easy to keep the captured sabercats alive once they lost the battle.\n\nNow, the hold has a bellyful of disarmed (but hostile) milodan captives to deal with, and the decision falls on Ula’s shoulders.
She looks to you for advice.

You could give council for mercy, suggesting that they be released into the wilds to live out the rest of their lives in the bitter chill, hiding from their Pyrite Industries debtors. It might not be pleasant, but they’d be largely free - something better than what they probably deserve.

You could council Ula toward harshness, suggesting that the up-gunned barbarians fate they expected to subject the korgonne to: a life deep under the ice in the hold’s mines, digging up riches they’ll never get to enjoy. The moment they invaded the hold, they ripped up their leases on liberty.

Or you could offer a middle ground solution: put the milodans to work in the mines as punishment for the damage they’ve caused. Then allow those who aren’t a risk to leave for the wilds - at the korgonne’s discretion, of course.

original: You let her know that it won’t be easy, but you’ll try.

A regal-looking cluster of korgonne passes by shortly after. At its regimented center is the chieftain, dressed in full expeditionary gear with a bit of ceremonial flair and surrounded by a half-dozen stiff-backed guards. Traditional spears and bows are the order of the day, ornamented with inlaid savicite gems and intricate scrollwork. The closest korgs glower at you while their cohorts do their best to pretend you don’t exist. The Chieftain himself comes to a lumbering stop next to his daughter. A heated exchange ensues, both royal korgs trading impassioned whispers like spearthrusts.

Judging by the expression on Ula’s face, she takes the worst of it. The old dog cleanly shatters whatever argument she was making like glass. Her tail sags in defeat, finally silenced by a dismissive wave of her father’s palm. Nodding gruffly, the Chief barks a command to his retinue, and they resume marching, leaving Ula to wallow in defeat. She stumbles back against the wall next to her as her father leaves, lacing her fingers together in raw nervousness

The teary-eyed korgonne turns to you for comfort, drawing you in to one last, fluffy hug. Minutes tick by, locked in her embrace. She sniffles a time or two before glancing back up in bleary-eyed determination. “"+({var0}()?"You need to go. The Chief doesn’t move that fast, but I can’t risk you losing him in the snow.":"You need go. Chieftain not move that fast but no take chance. No lose in snow.")+"”

original: Ula releases you into the snowfields of Uveto.
Next
Chieftain
Tracking

The Chieftain’s retinue isn’t as easy to track through the snow as you might have expected. They are obviously practiced at surviving the rugged tundras of Uvetan wilderness. Their path avoids softer snowpack where tracks might be more evident, moving instead to less malleable ice and rock. When they do leave tracks, the rearguard brushes them down with some kind of tool to conceal the shapes of their footprints. If you hadn’t had the opportunity to follow them from the very start, you’d never have noticed their track.

For well over one hour, you trudge after the fluffy procession, enduring Uveto’s hellish weather as best you can. You thought yourself stealthy, but more than once, you catch the sharp eye of a distant korgonne staring back at you impassively. The guards make no move to stop you, but neither do they care for your presence. You’re an outsider, a distant one who they consider to be trifling in matters beyond [pc.hisHer] ken.

You keep your distance. When the trekking dog-folk come to a stop in the shadow of an icy cliffside, you set up a short ways away: close enough to keep an eye on the proceedings but far away enough not to draw attention to yourself, should a keen scout happen to discover your vantage point.
Next
Milodans
Ambush
You are not prepared for the arrival of the Milodans. Neither is Ula’s father.

The saber-cats appear at the crest of the ridge with snarling grins and full sets of modern military equipment - including body armor. A score of the up-armed milos leap from the cliff to an apparent demise only for the blue-tinged exhausts of their jetpacks to grant them a graceful landing in snow below. In their hands are military-grade weapons of every shape and size: heavy missile launchers, railguns, lasers, and even a multitude of grenades. Fizzing shields are apparent around them, as are the trademarked stamps of Pyrite Heavy Industries logos.
Uh oh.
“Foolish korgs!” the leader bellows. His compatriots advance, arrows sparking from the invisible barriers that surround them. “So easy to fool, and so easy to kill!” He bellows cruelly.

Ula was right. The Chieftain doesn’t stand a chance. You doubt you can save him; there’s simply too many of the well-armed barbarians. Perhaps you could return to the hold to warn them of what’s coming. You doubt this is an isolated incident. Then again, you promised Ula you would watch over her father.
Do you try to save Ula’s father or warn the hold?
Warn Hold
Save Chief
Warn the Hold
Milodans

You retreat for the hold before the growing army of felines discover you. The haunting sight of two bodyguards falling to explosive blasts haunts your frenzied run back. Thoughts of cowardice chase you more effectively than any foe, at war with the pragmatic knowledge that you could not have change the outcome in the slightest. Still, defeat of any kind carries its own unique sting.

The rumble of whining engines and the bass hum of antigravity repulsors interrupt your thoughts. A flat-bottomed transport craft wooshes past you to the north. It holds more milodans than you saw back at the ambush - at least forty - and Ula’s father lies near the front, cuffed and unconscious. A double-barreled gun on the back houses a grinning milo in thick goggles. Artillery like that could punch a hole straight through the hold’s wall - or obliterate the door outright.
Another transport follows. There’s an army of the crude cat-men, and they’re buzzing straight to Korg’ii Hold.
You run faster.
Next
Milodans
With a roar of challenge, you crest the hill you concealed yourself behind and rush for the assembled milodans. Puffs of snow splash behind you, churned by your impelling fury.

The long-toothed cats barely react to your presence. Two of the Chieftain’s guards fall to explosive charges while the ring of advancing barbarians closes in, stun-rods in hand. At their leader’s direction, two of the least-equipped warriors break off to intercept you. Apparently you aren’t worth the presence of heavier weaponry.
Nevertheless, you’ll have to fight!
Fight!

Your stance is wobbly and shaken - not because you’re wounded or you’re too exhausted to keep going, but because you’re just so horny. The Milodan in front of you look so delicious in [enemy.hisHer] armor, and you can smell [enemy.hisHer] overbearing musk from where you are. Well... you tried. You don’t know how the korgonne could possibly defeat a force like that, but you hope they manage.
Next
To the Hold

You rush back through thickening snows, hoping against hope that the korgs’ stout walls and even stouter wills can prove a match for snarling muscle and blast-belching firearms. If the milodans get a chance to settle in... there will be no dislodging them - not without an army and significant bloodshed.
You run faster.
Next
Korg’ii Hold

Billowing clouds of smoke wreathe the distant spire long before you’re close enough to discern the source. They’re dark and black, twisting up like burnt, skeletal fingertips, trying to drag the hold down into the icy mires below. No matter how fast you move, it doesn’t seem fast enough. The bustling tribal city is burning.
You crest a hilltop.

Korg’ii Hold’s perfectly concealed, fluted entrance lies in a thousand shattered pieces, scattered amongst the blackened snow. Stolen spears lie stacked in heaps, their owners nowhere to be seen. The distant snaps of small arms fire sputter like popping corn from within, echoing across the tundra. The gateway may be lost, but the korgs must be fighting on inside!
A fresh plume of smoke erupts from a concealed window.
You rush in, grateful the invaders didn’t think to post a rearguard. To battle!
Next

So he does it for you: a rough, forceful paw slaps you on the forehead, then grips you on your scalp, as he tips your head back. You wince in pain as your neck creaks through your injuries; you hiss through clenched teeth as you’re forced to look your assailant in the eye. From your angle, you make out his thick, sturdy form, pressing against his armor, and your eyes fall onto his own calm-but-stern gaze.

He kneels down to you, and his other paw crassly grips you by your jaw. He turns your head from side to side, and he tuts at your imperfections and bruises.
